quick_facts.label: Conducting body
quick_facts.value: Staff Selection Commission (SSC), Government of India.

quick_facts.label: What SSC JE recruits for
quick_facts.value: An open competitive examination for Junior Engineer posts in Civil, Mechanical and Electrical disciplines in Government of India organisations/offices.

quick_facts.label: Qualification route
quick_facts.value: Eligibility is post-specific. In the 2025 notice, the listed routes generally used a relevant engineering degree or three-year diploma; some posts additionally required relevant post-qualification experience.

quick_facts.label: Examination structure
quick_facts.value: The official SSC scheme specifies two computer-based papers: Paper I has General Intelligence and Reasoning, General Awareness, and a chosen General Engineering discipline; Paper II is the chosen General Engineering discipline.

quick_facts.label: Pay context
quick_facts.value: For the 2025 recruitment notice, SSC described the advertised JE posts as Group B (Non-Gazetted), Non-Ministerial, Pay Level 6: ₹35,400–₹1,12,400 in the 7th CPC Pay Matrix. This is a dated notice-level pay context, not a guarantee for later cycles or every employer-specific posting.

quick_facts.label: Most recent confirmed 2025-cycle milestone
quick_facts.value: SSC published the First Round of Tentative Allocation on July 9, 2026. SSC states that marks from both papers were used for that allocation process.

sections.heading: What SSC JE is for
paragraphs: SSC JE is the Staff Selection Commission’s competitive recruitment examination for Junior Engineer roles in central-government organisations and offices. The examination covers Civil, Mechanical and Electrical engineering streams; the exact organisations, posts, vacancies, physical/medical requirements where applicable, and qualifications are set out in each cycle’s SSC notice.

For example, SSC’s 2025 notice described the recruitment as being for Group B (Non-Gazetted), Non-Ministerial Junior Engineer posts. Candidates should treat the notice for the cycle they apply to—not a prior-year list of posts or vacancies—as authoritative.
bullets: Use SSC JE when seeking entry into eligible central-government Junior Engineer recruitment in the notified engineering discipline.
Do not assume that every listed department accepts the same diploma, degree, age range, experience record, or disability category: these are post-specific in the notification.

sections.heading: Eligibility: check the post, discipline and cut-off date
paragraphs: SSC JE eligibility is not a single universal rule. The 2025 notice lists essential qualifications separately by organisation and post. Relevant Civil, Electrical or Mechanical engineering degrees and three-year diplomas are common routes, but certain notified posts require work experience in addition to a diploma. The notice states that required experience must be obtained after completing the specified educational qualification; internship, training and research experience undertaken while acquiring the qualification do not count as the required experience.

Age limits also vary by post. The 2025 notice included posts with maximum ages such as 30 or 32 years, subject to the notice’s applicable cut-off date and relaxation provisions. Applicants should match their exact qualification, engineering branch, experience and age against the table for each post before submitting preferences.

The 2025 notice also explains that engineering programmes are generally not permitted through open and distance learning under the cited UGC regulations, subject to the stated historical IGNOU exception. Qualification recognition and documentary proof are checked by the user department during document verification.
bullets: Read the post table rather than relying only on the exam’s broad title.
Keep original degree/diploma mark sheets, certificates and, where required, post-qualification experience evidence ready for document verification.
For Border Roads Organisation or any other post with additional standards, review the specific physical and medical conditions before choosing that post.

sections.heading: Exam format and scoring stages
paragraphs: SSC’s published JE scheme uses two computer-based examination papers. Paper I is a two-hour test comprising 50 questions/50 marks in General Intelligence and Reasoning, 50 questions/50 marks in General Awareness, and 100 questions/100 marks in one General Engineering option: Civil & Structural, Electrical, or Mechanical. Paper II is a two-hour computer-based test with 100 questions for 300 marks in the selected General Engineering option. Eligible scribe candidates receive the extended duration stated by SSC.

The 2025 cycle illustrates the sequence: SSC conducted Paper I in computer-based mode, declared its result to shortlist candidates for Paper II, and later used marks from both papers for the First Round of Tentative Allocation. SSC reported normalization for Paper I because it was held in multiple shifts and described additional normalization between Mechanical and Electrical Paper II scores for common posts.
bullets: Choose and prepare the General Engineering option that corresponds to the discipline and posts for which you are eligible.
Treat current cut-offs, negative-marking rules, normalization procedures and qualifying thresholds as notification-specific; verify them in the active SSC JE notice and its later updates.

sections.heading: How to apply and track a cycle
paragraphs: Apply only through SSC’s official platform when an SSC JE notice is open. SSC’s candidate guidance states that One-Time Registration (OTR) is mandatory before applying for Commission examinations. The 2025 JE notice required online application through SSC’s new website and set a separate application window, payment deadline and correction window; those dates are historical and must not be reused for a future cycle.

SSC’s 2026–27 tentative calendar listed SSC JE 2026 with a tentative March 2026 advertisement period, April 2026 closing period, and May–June 2026 Paper I window. Because the calendar is explicitly tentative, candidates must rely on the actual advertisement, corrigenda and notice-board updates for operative dates, fees, vacancies, eligibility cut-offs, examination dates and admit-card instructions.

After Paper II, SSC may require candidates to submit an option-cum-preference for posts/organisations through Candidate Login. In the 2025 cycle, SSC made this a required step for candidates who appeared in Paper II and stated that candidates who did not submit preferences within the specified window would not be considered for the final merit list/final selection.
bullets: Create or verify SSC OTR before the deadline; do not wait until the last day of an application window.
Download and save the cycle notice, submitted application, payment confirmation, correction confirmation, admit-card notices and answer-key/scorecard records.
Submit post and organisation preferences only after checking qualification, age, experience and any job-specific physical or medical requirements.

sections.heading: Preparation approach aligned to the official scheme
paragraphs: Build preparation around the two-paper structure. Paper I requires three parallel areas—reasoning, general awareness and the selected engineering discipline—while Paper II concentrates entirely on the selected Civil & Structural, Electrical or Mechanical engineering syllabus. This makes discipline-specific technical revision central to performance across both papers.

Use SSC’s official scheme and the current cycle’s detailed notice as the primary checklist. Practise computer-based questions under the two-hour time limit, separately track accuracy in Paper I’s non-technical portions, and devote substantial revision time to the General Engineering branch chosen for both papers. Review official answer-key and result notices after the examination, because SSC has published final answer keys, response sheets and marks for completed cycles for limited access periods.
bullets: Make a topic tracker for the three Paper I components and the Paper II technical syllabus.
Use timed mock sessions matching the paper durations stated by SSC.
Prioritise official notices, scheme documents, answer-key notices and prior SSC materials over unofficial claims about pattern changes or cut-offs.

sections.heading: What happens after the examination
paragraphs: Qualifying SSC JE can lead to consideration for notified central-government Junior Engineer posts, but selection is not simply a pass result. SSC’s 2025 process shortlisted candidates from Paper I for Paper II, collected post/organisation preferences from Paper II candidates, and made tentative allocation using merit and the applicable preference process. The relevant user department subsequently handles required document verification and appointment-related checks.

For the 2025 cycle, SSC’s July 9, 2026 First Round of Tentative Allocation notice states that 15,607 candidates had been shortlisted for Paper II, 14,062 appeared in Paper II, and 11,052 candidates who submitted online preferences were considered further. These figures describe that particular cycle only and should not be used to estimate future competition or vacancies.

The pay level, employer, work location and post conditions can differ by the actual vacancy. The 2025 notice’s Level 6 pay matrix reference is useful context, but applicants should verify the current notice and the eventual appointing organisation’s conditions.
bullets: A good Paper I score alone does not complete the process; Paper II, merit treatment and required preference submission matter.
Keep eligibility evidence available through document verification; a qualification or experience mismatch can cancel candidature.
Review allocation, identity-verification and user-department communications after SSC publishes results.
